original_hypothesis.title: addManagedNFT Allows Duplicate NFT Entries Creating Double-Withdrawal and Release Issues
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: addManagedNFT only checks that the contract owns the NFT but does not check whether the NFT is already in the ManagedNFTs array. If the owner calls addManagedNFT(nft) twice, the same NFT address appears twice in ManagedNFTs. During withdrawFromManagedNFTs, the first withdrawal drains the NFT's balance; the second withdrawal for the same NFT gets 0 (wasting gas). When releaseManagedNFT is called, only one occurrence is removed (the loop breaks), leaving a stale duplicate entry. Subsequent withdrawal cycles hit the stale entry (NFT no longer owned), causing reverts.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Combined with the require(true) bug in releaseManagedNFT, this creates a compounding state corruption. The stale entry from an incomplete duplicate removal cannot be detected or removed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ManagedNFTs.push(nftContract) without checking for existing entry; no deduplication

preconditions: Owner calls addManagedNFT with same NFT address more than once
Contract owns the NFT at time of each call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No duplicate check before push to ManagedNFTs
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: releaseManagedNFT with break only removes first occurrence, leaving stale duplicate
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Mapping to track managed NFT membership, or duplicate check before push

analysis_questions: Can addManagedNFT be called for the same NFT twice?
What is the impact of a stale ManagedNFTs entry on withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Call addManagedNFT(nft) twice, verify ManagedNFTs contains duplicate
Release the NFT, verify one entry remains
Attempt withdrawal cycle, verify revert on stale entry

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner accidentally adds same NFT twice. When releasing it, releaseManagedNFT breaks after finding and removing the first occurrence. A stale duplicate entry remains. Next withdrawal cycle tries to withdraw from the released (unowned) NFT and reverts, blocking revenue collection.
prerequisites: Owner adds same NFT address to ManagedNFTs more than once

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Withdrawal DoS blocking revenue collection from all managed NFTs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders affected by blocked distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult — no way to remove stale entry without owning the NFT again

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for duplicate addManagedNFT calls

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via ManagedNFTs array inspection for duplicates

validation_result.mechanism: Missing duplicate check in addManagedNFT + incomplete removal in releaseManagedNFT
validation_result.impact_summary: Duplicate NFT entries in ManagedNFTs array cause stale entries after release, leading to withdrawal DoS when withdrawFromManagedNFTs attempts to interact with an unowned NFT.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The addManagedNFT function at LiquidInfrastructureERC20.sol only checks that the contract owns the NFT (`nftOwner == address(this)`) but performs no check to see if the NFT address is already present in ManagedNFTs. This allows the owner to call addManagedNFT(nft) multiple times for the same NFT, creating duplicates. When releaseManagedNFT is called, the loop uses `break` after finding and removing the first occurrence via swap-and-pop, leaving subsequent duplicate entries intact. Since `require(true, ...)` is a no-op (always passes), the function silently succeeds even with remaining duplicates. After the NFT is transferred out, the stale entry points to an NFT no longer owned by the contract. When withdrawFromManagedNFTs iterates to the stale entry, it calls withdrawBalancesTo on the released NFT, which has access control requiring the caller to be owner or approved. Since the ERC20 contract no longer owns the NFT, this call reverts, blocking the entire withdrawal cycle at that index.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Three distinct code defects combine: (1) No deduplication in addManagedNFT, (2) Only first-occurrence removal in releaseManagedNFT due to break, (3) require(true) that never validates removal success. All are directly visible in the source code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check for whether nftContract is already in ManagedNFTs. The ownership check passes on subsequent calls since the contract still owns it, allowing duplicate entries.
The `break` statement exits after removing the first occurrence, leaving duplicate entries. The `require(true, ...)` always passes, providing no validation that all entries were removed.
When iterating over ManagedNFTs and encountering a stale entry (released NFT), withdrawBalancesTo will revert due to access control (caller is no longer owner), blocking the entire withdrawal cycle.
Access control check that will revert when called by the LiquidInfrastructureERC20 contract on a released NFT it no longer owns.

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls addManagedNFT(nftAddr) - ownership check passes, nftAddr pushed to ManagedNFTs
2. Owner calls addManagedNFT(nftAddr) again - ownership check passes again (contract still owns it), nftAddr pushed again creating duplicate
4. Owner calls releaseManagedNFT(nftAddr, recipient) - NFT transferred to recipient, loop finds first occurrence at index i, swap-and-pop removes it, break exits loop
5. ManagedNFTs still contains one nftAddr entry (the stale duplicate)
6. require(true) passes silently without detecting the remaining duplicate
7. Anyone calls withdrawFromManagedNFTs() - loop reaches the stale nftAddr entry
8. withdrawFrom.withdrawBalancesTo(withdrawERC20s, address(this)) is called on the released NFT
9. _isApprovedOrOwner check in withdrawBalancesTo fails because the ERC20 contract no longer owns the NFT
10. Transaction reverts, blocking all withdrawals from managed NFTs
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: addManagedNFT (no duplicate check) → duplicate entry created → releaseManagedNFT (break after first removal + require(true) no-op) → stale entry persists → withdrawFromManagedNFTs → withdrawBalancesTo on unowned NFT → revert on _isApprovedOrOwner check

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script shows addManagedNFT is called via transferNftToErc20AndManage helper function. No duplicate-prevention logic at the script level. The deployment script deploys new NFTs and manages them, but doesn't guard against double-add.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is fully reproducible: (1) Owner deploys LiquidInfrastructureERC20 and a LiquidInfrastructureNFT, (2) Owner transfers NFT to ERC20 contract, (3) Owner calls addManagedNFT(nftAddr) twice - both calls succeed since ownership check passes both times, (4) ManagedNFTs = [nftAddr, nftAddr], (5) Owner calls releaseManagedNFT(nftAddr, someAddress) - NFT transferred, first entry removed via break, (6) ManagedNFTs = [nftAddr] (stale entry), (7) withdrawFromManagedNFTs() reverts when trying to call withdrawBalancesTo on the now-unowned NFT. Recovery is difficult - the owner would need to reacquire the NFT (get it transferred back), add it again, then release it, which would only remove one more entry. The require(true) bug means there's no detection of the stale entry problem.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The defect requires owner error (calling addManagedNFT twice for the same NFT), but once triggered: (1) Withdrawal DoS blocks all revenue collection from ALL managed NFTs until the stale entry is somehow resolved, (2) All token holders are affected by blocked distributions since no revenue can be collected, (3) Recovery requires reacquiring ownership of the released NFT which may not be possible if the recipient refuses to return it. The compound effect with the require(true) bug means there's no warning when release doesn't fully clean up.
recommended_mitigations: Add a mapping(address => bool) isManagedNFT to track membership, and check it in addManagedNFT before pushing: require(!isManagedNFT[nftContract], 'NFT already managed')
Set isManagedNFT[nftContract] = true in addManagedNFT and isManagedNFT[nftContract] = false in releaseManagedNFT
Replace require(true, ...) with a boolean flag: bool found = false; set found = true inside the loop when match is found; require(found, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs')
Alternative: use the isManagedNFT mapping to skip the loop entirely and use a different removal strategy
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidInfrastructureNFT and LiquidInfrastructureERC20. 2. Transfer NFT to ERC20 contract. 3. Call addManagedNFT(nftAddr) twice. 4. Verify ManagedNFTs.length == 2 and both entries are nftAddr. 5. Call releaseManagedNFT(nftAddr, someAddress). 6. Verify ManagedNFTs.length == 1 and ManagedNFTs[0] == nftAddr (stale). 7. Call withdrawFromAllManagedNFTs() and verify it reverts with 'caller is not the owner of the Account token and is not approved either'.
uncertainties: Exact line numbers may vary slightly depending on formatting/comments in the original source
additional_testing_needed: Test that addManagedNFT can be called twice with the same address
Test that releaseManagedNFT leaves a stale entry when duplicates exist
Test that withdrawFromManagedNFTs reverts on the stale entry
Test recovery path: can the owner re-add the NFT after reacquiring it to fix the array?
